title: Through-tenon and wedged through-tenon
subtitle: Cutting, fitting, and wedging a through-tenon joint by hand
excerpt: A step-by-step technique entry covering the through-tenon joint: marking the mortise and tenon, boring out waste, chopping the mortise walls, sawing the tenon cheeks and shoulders, dry-fitting, and driving a hardwood wedge to lock the joint. Applicable to handles, rungs, stretchers, and any joint where the tenon must be visible and permanent.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for mortise-and-tenon joinery; Victorian joinery manuals for wedging methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: A tenon that passes completely through the mortise piece and projects beyond the far face. The projecting end can be wedged, pegged, or trimmed flush.
The socket cut to receive the tenon. In a through-mortise, the socket runs completely through the piece; in a stopped mortise, it stops short of the far face.

The slot cut by a saw blade. In a wedged through-tenon, one or two saw kerfs are cut into the protruding end of the tenon; the wedges are driven into these slots to spread the tenon against the mortise walls.

content.text: Keep both hands behind the cutting edge. Secure the workpiece firmly in a vice before chopping the mortise; do not hold it free in the hand.
is the most durable of the hand-cut mortise-and-tenon variants. Because the tenon passes completely through the piece, it can be wedged, making it resistant to pull-out without glue. The
for the wedge must be cut in the right position: central for a single wedge, or one at each side for a double-wedged joint.
Set the marking gauge to one-third the thickness of the tenon piece; this is the standard tenon thickness ratio. Mark the
width and position on both faces and both edges of the mortise piece. A through-mortise must be marked on both faces so the chisel can work from each side in turn.

Bore out most of the waste with a brace and bit set to a diameter slightly smaller than the mortise width. Work from both faces to prevent blowout. Then chop to the marked lines with the mortise chisel: chop from the front face to just past centre, then turn the piece and chop from the back face to meet the first cut. Lever out the waste chips with the chisel bevel.

Mark the tenon shoulders with a marking gauge on all four faces of the tenon piece. The tenon length must exceed the mortise piece thickness by 8 to 10 mm, to give enough projection for the wedge. Saw the cheeks first with the tenon saw, keeping the cut on the waste side of the gauge line. Then saw the shoulders.

The tenon should enter the mortise with firm hand pressure and project 8 to 10 mm. With the joint assembled, saw the wedge kerf into the projecting end: 15 mm deep, centred, parallel to the long axis of the mortise. Cut the wedge from a short offcut of hardwood: 60 mm long, tapering from 5 mm to 1 mm over the length. Drive the wedge into the kerf with the mallet, tightening the joint as it spreads. Trim the protruding tenon and wedge flush or leave proud.

items.symptom: Wedge splits the tenon end when driven
items.cause: Kerf cut too close to one edge of the tenon, leaving too little wood at one side; or wedge too wide and driven too hard
items.fix: Centre the kerf carefully before sawing. Drive the wedge in stages: a few taps, then check. If the tenon shows a crack starting, stop, draw bore a small peg hole through the joint before driving the wedge again, so the peg prevents further splitting.
